item.title: Proper process ensures timely services: Opa
item.description: PROPER procurement processes and principles must be adhered to for the timely delivery of goods and services to the people, says Finance Minister Thomas Opa. “It is time we improve our work and use systems that can improve work flow,” Opa said.
item.content:encoded:
PROPER procurement processes and principles must be adhered to for the timely delivery of goods and services to the people, says Finance Minister Thomas Opa.

“It is time we improve our work and use systems that can improve work flow,” Opa said.

“Public procurement must not become cumbersome, but rather promote good governance and service delivery.

“Some contracts that are awarded are held in procurement process for 18 to 24 months.

“So we need to look at reducing this lengthy process.”

He made the remarks during the launching of the National Procurement Commission (NPC) corporate plan (2026-2030), and management action plan (2026) in Port Moresby on Friday.

“The corporate plan launched today will strengthen NPC’s institutional capacity and improve procurement processes for the better,” he added.

Opa urged the NPC to implement its electronic procurement system.

“A key component of our reform agenda is the progressive adoption of electronic procurement,” he said.

“Electronic procurement must be a priority as it will promote transparency and accountability, while also reducing delays and minimising risks.

“I would also like to see the amendments of the NPC Act in my time.”

Opa also urged the NPC management and board to submit to his office plans and progress of their work to be presented in Parliament.

NPC chief executive officer Joe Sapa said the commission would focus on strengthening its current system, while also implement the electronic procurement system, now that the five-year corporate plan has been launched.

“Public procurement is a crucial for national development and service delivery,” Sapa said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Plastic waste audit training conducted
item.description: FOLLOWING the recent declaration of New Ireland’s Kafkaf village as an environmental hazard, a plastic waste audit training-of- trainers course was conducted in the province to strengthen community-driven responses to plastic pollution. The two-day training, led by the Conservation and Environment Protection Authority (Cepa) in partnership with Ailan Awareness and the Lolieng Sustainable Programme
item.content:encoded:
FOLLOWING the recent declaration of New Ireland’s Kafkaf village as an environmental hazard, a plastic waste audit training-of- trainers course was conducted in the province to strengthen community-driven responses to plastic pollution.

The two-day training, led by the Conservation and Environment Protection Authority (Cepa) in partnership with Ailan Awareness and the Lolieng Sustainable Programme, equipped six trainers with the skills and tools needed to collect plastic waste data using a mobile application.

The digital platform allows trainers to gather real-time field data using a standardised method to track waste types, quantities and sources.

Cepa consultant Ill Pilgah said the system replaces paper-based records, reducing the burden of carrying documents and printing equipment.

“It also allows for on the spot tracking, making it efficient for sea travel in between coastal islands,” he said.

The data collected would help determine key indicators such as the volume of plastic waste generated per household, the composition of waste by weight, and the extent of plastic pollution in coastal and marine environments.

Pilgah explained that the information would support local government to develop evidence-based policies, including targeted bans on certain plastic products, while also identifying gaps in waste management systems and guiding better investment and collection strategies.

He further highlighted that the audit is expected to have a strong impact on coastal communities by encouraging ownership and active participation in managing and reducing plastic pollution.

Meanwhile, Ailan Awareness representative John Aini thanked Cepa for the initiative and emphasised on the impact it would have on tourism in the province.

“This programme would motivate communities to actively participate in data-driven solutions resulting in improved environmental health and a boost for the tourism industry, the plastic waste audit encourages beach clean-ups that would restore our beaches as a major tourism attraction,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: East Sepik, Manus sign agreement to boost health services
item.description: THE East Sepik Health Authority (ESPHA) has partnered with six district development authorities (DDAs) and the Manus Health Authority (MPHA) in an agreement aimed at boosting medical services across the region. Signed on Friday, the collaboration focuses on strengthening health systems and improving the quality of both primary and tertiary health services for the rural majority.
item.content:encoded:
THE East Sepik Health Authority (ESPHA) has partnered with six district development authorities (DDAs) and the Manus Health Authority (MPHA) in an agreement aimed at boosting medical services across the region.

Signed on Friday, the collaboration focuses on strengthening health systems and improving the quality of both primary and tertiary health services for the rural majority.

Health secretary Pascoe Kase highlighted that these service-level agreements (SLAs) were a vital tool for financial accountability.

“We sign this agreement and it details exactly what that money is used for in health in a particular district,” Kase said.

He noted that 20 per cent of district resources were legally meant for health delivery, and these agreements ensured funds were spent on targeted areas rather than in an ad hoc manner.

ESPHA chief executive officer Mathew Kaluvia described the signing as a “deliberate step” toward closer alignment of the provincial health system.

He emphasised that the SLAs clarified duties and expectations, paving the way for better responses to epidemics and natural disasters.

“The SLA is the way forward to improve health service delivery in the province,” Kaluvia added, noting that DDAs look after the majority of the population at the district level.

The partnership includes a unique memorandum of agreement (MoA) with Manus to assist remote island communities.

Under this arrangement, ESPHA will cater to patients from the Wuvulu and Western islands and release medical specialists to Manus, provided MPHA covers the operational costs, including a K2,000 monthly fee for patients accommodated at Boram Hospital. Manus Executive Council’s health chairman Hans Poiou explained that the Western islands were geographically closer to East Sepik, making logistics difficult.

“It is good that we sign an agreement between us so we can help each other,” Poiou said.

East Sepik Governor Allan Bird attributed the progress to consistent management, which recently secured K50 million funding from the World Bank for new facilities.

“I want to thank our various DDAs who have recognised that and are now going to sign those agreements,” Governor Bird said.

International Trade and Investment Minister and member for Yangoru Saussia Richard Maru confirmed that the Yangoru-Saussia DDA was already acting on the agreement, with plans to build six new health centres and a Level Four district hospital.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PM launches policy on displaced persons
item.description: AN Internally Displaced Persons (IDP) policy that aims to address the issue of people displaced by natural and man-made hazards, climate change and other development-related displacements was launched on Friday. Prime Minister James Marape said directives were given to the Provincial and Local Level Government Affairs Department (DPLGA) in 2017 to formulate an IDP policy and it took them 10 years.
item.content:encoded:
AN Internally Displaced Persons (IDP) policy that aims to address the issue of people displaced by natural and man-made hazards, climate change and other development-related displacements was launched on Friday.

Prime Minister James Marape said directives were given to the Provincial and Local Level Government Affairs Department (DPLGA) in 2017 to formulate an IDP policy and it took them 10 years.

He was critical of the time it took to develop the policy, describing it as “inexcusable”.

Marape said the Government directive took a decade to be implemented which was “quite embarrassing”.

“Those serving in public service, we have no time to waste. When instructions are given, work with haste,” Marape told DPLGA staff at the launch.

“Displacements are happening everyday as we speak. Even in the absence of policies, there are congruent policies available for us to address.

“We don’t have to wait for a policy to happen 10 years later, lives have been lost and we are playing marbles in Waigani.

“When policy instructions come out, don’t waste your time.”

DPLGA Minister Soroi Eoe said this was the country’s first IDP policy that outlined strategic guidelines to address the basic needs of those displaced, identify and offer sustainable relocation options and programmes and seek to engage key stakeholders and development partners to collaborate with the government to minimise displacement, provide humanitarian assistance and find solutions.

DPLGA secretary Philip Leo said data and information were gathered across 17 provinces to assess the various causes of internal displacement before developing the policy.

He said in 2024, more than 214,000 people were affected by natural and human-induced hazards, with over 30,000 displaced.

“Over 20,000 remain in protracted displacement, unable to achieve durable solutions.

“Displacement continues to undermine livelihoods, land security, safety and access to basic services,” he said.

Marape urged Eoe to check Madang’s Manam restoration authority’s status, East Sepik’s Kadovar resettlement process, Enga’s Mulitaka restoration authority, to check police and community development on the status of tribal fights affected resettlement, check the National Capital District on the recent eviction resettlement plans and with Climate Change Development Authority on climate change related resettlements.

“If you want to maintain your designation in the public service, all departments must get to work.

“All State agencies must support DPLGA and work with the provincial governments and partners as they know which people are being displaced by natural disaster, man-made causes or other ways,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Catholic group calls for proper reforms before national elections
item.description: THE Catholic Professionals Society of PNG has called for proper electoral reforms to be implemented in time for General Election 2027 (GE27). Society president Paul Harricknen said: “GE27 will test our nation again.
item.content:encoded:
THE Catholic Professionals Society of PNG has called for proper electoral reforms to be implemented in time for General Election 2027 (GE27).

Society president Paul Harricknen said: “GE27 will test our nation again.

“We have seen the wounds of 2012, 2017, and 2022.

“Without genuine electoral reform, without credible census and voter enrolment, without proper funding and preparation, and without honest discharge of national duty by public servants, election officials, and security personnel, we risk deeper instability and an undesirable outcome again.

“Free, fair, and safe elections are not just words — they are foundations of justice.

“We must demand accountability before it is too late.

“Democracy without integrity collapses into chaos.”

Harricknen urged people to understand the importance of the country’s electoral processes to prevent it from being abused for political gain.

He said every Papua New Guinean had a responsibility to ensure the success of GE27.

“Today, PNG stands at a crossroads,” he said.

“We can continue normalising corruption and money politics or we can choose moral courage.

“When these are abandoned for greed and political survival, the nation loses its soul.

“The reset of PNG will not begin in Parliament.

“It will begin in our consciences and our families.”

He also called on the members of the professional network to play their part in the preparation for GE27.

“The Church cannot retreat into comfort.

“Civil society cannot remain fragmented.

“If we believe we are Pilgrims of Hope, then hope must be visible in our conduct, credible in our leadership, and courageous in our action,” Harricknen said.

Meanwhile, acting Electoral Commissioner Margaret Vagi confirmed the tentative dates for GE27 as:

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Elections in crisis, says ex-commissioner
item.description: Papua New Guinea’s elections are in crisis, warns Sam Koim. Koim, the former Internal Revenue Commission commissioner who resigned to contest the Dei by-election and came in second, said the integrity of elections was not just an administrative detail but the foundation of democracy itself.
item.content:encoded:
Papua New Guinea’s elections are in crisis, warns Sam Koim.

Koim, the former Internal Revenue Commission commissioner who resigned to contest the Dei by-election and came in second, said the integrity of elections was not just an administrative detail but the foundation of democracy itself.

“If the system is weak, contested, and vulnerable, every law and policy that follows rests on shaky ground,” he said.

“In some areas, polling booths are ‘effectively hijacked by a few individuals who proceed to mark ballot papers in bulk’.

“With ghost names on the common roll continuing to distort the franchise, diluting the power of genuine voters.

“These are not merely administrative flaws, they are democratic vulnerabilities.”

Koim said that the cost of elections only added to the strain.

“The Electoral Commission has indicated that around K500 millions of taxpayer funds will be needed for the 2027 National General Election.

“Once post-election operational costs like vehicles, accommodation, and security are factored in, the total could approach K600 million.

“Candidates and their supporters also spend heavily, with estimates of K500 million to K600 million.

“When public and private costs are combined, PNG is effectively running a billion-kina electoral economy every five years, and yet, for all these spendings, disputes remain the rule rather than the exception.”

Koim said that the data was plain from 2012 to 2022, between 87 per cent and 95 per cent of all seats in PNG’s last three national elections were contested in the Court of Disputed Returns.

“Election petitions are meant to be exceptional remedies. Instead, they have become routine, burdening the judiciary and prolonging political uncertainty,” he said.

Koim said families were divided, communities polarised, property destroyed and lives lost.

 “These are real human consequences flowing from a system that too often produces contested legitimacy,” he said.

Koim called for change and is advocating for a modern electoral system anchored on biometric voter verification and automated voting and tabulation, with voter-verifiable audit trails.

“Biometric systems can ensure that only eligible citizens vote, and only once.

“Automation reduces excessive human handling, limiting opportunities for manipulation.

“Faster counting, can ease prolonged uncertainty and prevent tension from escalating after elections,” he said.

Koim used India as proof that such systems can work.

“PNG’s entire electorate is comparable to a small province in India.

“If electronic voting and auditing can work across a subcontinent, they are achievable in PNG, provided they are adapted to our terrain, infrastructure, and legal framework,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Service providers get BPNG licences
item.description: THE Bank of Papua New Guinea (BPNG) has issued licence certificates to two payment service providers (PSP) and one microfinance institution – expanding access to safe, reliable and inclusive financial services for Papua New Guineans. BPNG governor Elizabeth Genia presented the licences to Digitec PNG Financial Services Limited and Omega Paymybills PNG Ltd, and a microfinance institution licence to
item.content:encoded:
THE Bank of Papua New Guinea (BPNG) has issued licence certificates to two payment service providers (PSP) and one microfinance institution – expanding access to safe, reliable and inclusive financial services for Papua New Guineans.

BPNG governor Elizabeth Genia presented the licences to Digitec PNG Financial Services Limited and Omega Paymybills PNG Ltd, and a microfinance institution licence to Lower OK Tedi Micro Bank Ltd in Port Moresby on Friday.

Genia said a PSP licence enabled financial institutions to provide users with the ability to securely store money, make payments, and transfer funds directly from their mobile phones without a traditional bank account.

“Similarly, microfinance takes deposits and provides lending to small businesses who lack access to traditional credit opportunities.”

Genia said that this development strengthened the country’s financial system while expanding opportunities for individuals, families and businesses.

“The expansion of licensed payment services means greater convenience, faster transactions, and improved access to digital financial services for our people, including those in the rural and remote areas,” she said.

Genia reminded the institutions that a licence was not merely a certificate, but a responsibility.

“As regulated entities, they are required to maintain strong oversight, effective governance and strict compliance with all regulatory requirements and Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing (AML/CTF) obligations,” she said.

“As licensed financial institutions, you form part of the first line of defence in protecting our financial system.

“Effective customer due diligence and a strong compliance culture must be embedded in your operations from day one.

“Weaknesses in this area expose your institution and the entire financial system to significant risks.

“Of course, this is only the start of a long collaboration.

“You have our full support in meeting these expectations.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Rosso sourcing US investment for ‘cheap and reliable’ energy
item.description: RELIABLE and cheap energy will boost the business activities and the economy, says Deputy Prime Minister John Rosso. In a meeting with the United States officials and investors in Hawaii, he told them to “put your money where your mouth is”.
item.content:encoded:
RELIABLE and cheap energy will boost the business activities and the economy, says Deputy Prime Minister John Rosso.

In a meeting with the United States officials and investors in Hawaii, he told them to “put your money where your mouth is”.  

Rosso told a dialogue session at the Pacific Investment, Security and Shared Prosperity summit in Honolulu, Hawaii, that the number of American businesses in PNG was low compared to other countries.

He encouraged the US government and businesses to invest more in PNG.

“How many American companies are in Papua New Guinea?  I can only think of two – ExxonMobil extracting oil and Newmont extracting gold,” he said.

“I can count these companies on one hand. You need to put your money where your mouth is.”

Rosso added that unreliable energy (electricity) supply was one of the main obstacles to investment in PNG.

“Good energy will attract good investment,” he said.

“Reliable cheap power can lead to, as an example, increased tourism, an industry which PNG and most of the Pacific depend on, which will result in a variety of spin-off businesses.

“Consistent and dependable electricity also allows us to set up factories for downstream processing in, for example, the fisheries and logging industries. One factory could create 5,000 jobs.

“This may be minuscule for America’s economy, but for PNG and other Pacific nations, those jobs would lead to GDP growth and make a huge difference to economic development in our countries and the region.”

The summit brought Pacific leaders together to advance regional investment and security cooperation to support the island nations.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG must change laws in six months: Kwa
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea has six months to pass four laws or face deeper financial isolation, the country’s justice chief told Lae business leaders last Friday. Dr Eric Kwa, chairman of the National Coordinating Committee (NCC) and secretary for Justice and Attorney-General, said that following its grey listing in Mexico City last month, the Financial Action Task Force (FATF) imposed 18 conditions, four o
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea has six months to pass four laws or face deeper financial isolation, the country’s justice chief told Lae business leaders last Friday.

Dr Eric Kwa, chairman of the National Coordinating Committee (NCC) and secretary for Justice and Attorney-General, said that following its grey listing in Mexico City last month, the Financial Action Task Force (FATF) imposed 18 conditions, four of which were flagged as urgent.

“This year, we are required to address only the four core conditions. But under those four, there are others that we also need to address,” he said.

Kwa said before PNG’s delegation flew to Mexico, Prime Minister James Marape committed the entire government to the plan.

“We took it to Cabinet. Cabinet endorsed the action plan, after which we sent it back to FATF,” he said.

“We asked the prime minister to commit to implementing this new set of action plans, too.”

The four urgent reforms are amending the Proceeds of Crime Act, overhauling the Criminal Code, passing a new Sanctions Act, and publishing the country’s National Risk Assessment on AML/CTF.

On the Proceeds of Crime Act, Kwa said the amendment was straightforward.

“They are one or two provisions that we need to work on, not the whole law, FATF wants us to use certain words that align with their standards so we just changed the wordings. We’ve already done it,” Kwa said.

On the Criminal Code, he said FATF found PNG’s laws were missing key definitions.

“They said our criminal code does not include certain anti-money laundering definitions,” Kwa said.

“So we are amending it to include what are known as predicate crimes, so that our laws can combat money laundering.”

On the Sanctions Act, a dedicated team will be stationed at the Prime Minister’s Office.

“We have a group of people there who work 24/7 to ensure the list is always up to date,” Kwa said.

“That is why, when they asked us, we said we could do it within six months.”

Wilson Onea, director of the Financial Analysis and Supervision Unit at the Bank of PNG, said the National Risk Assessment was almost complete.

“We are currently finalising it, after which we will hold a workshop with stakeholders, including the public and reporting entities,” he said.

“The FATF expects us to publish it by September this year.”

The NCC, comprising 23 agencies, has been operating for almost 10 years, guiding PNG off the grey list in 2016 after its first listing in 2014.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Bank expects no hindrance from grey-listing
item.description: KINA Bank does not expect the grey-listing of the country to affect its financial performance or hinder its strategic plans, says chief executive officer Ivan Vidovic. “Kina is working with regulators, government agencies, customers, and correspondent banks as part of the national response to address these issues,” Vidovich said.
item.content:encoded:
KINA Bank does not expect the grey-listing of the country to affect its financial performance or hinder its strategic plans, says chief executive officer Ivan Vidovic.

“Kina is working with regulators, government agencies, customers, and correspondent banks as part of the national response to address these issues,” Vidovich said.

He said Kina had launched its 2030 strategy, marking the transition into the next phase of growth as PNG’s challenger bank.

“In 2026, the bank plans to issue PNG’s first listed corporate bond under the new capital markets legislation-an important milestone for domestic financial market development,” he added.

“The bank expects earnings growth to continue in 2026, supported by diversified revenue uplift, ongoing market share gains, and a scheduled reduction in the statutory corporate tax rate for banking operations.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
